summary: Gruber analyzes Amazon's Kindle Fire press event, arguing it was brilliantly targeted at tech press who would filter the message to consumers. He breaks down how Bezos positioned the Kindle Fire specifically against the iPad using pricing psychology and a 'make money when you use it' narrative. The piece explains Amazon's deliberate strategy of limiting sales to countries where they have content deals, since the device is an accelerator for Amazon's media ecosystem rather than a standalone product. Gruber concludes that Amazon has a 'heads we win, tails you lose' strategy — they profit whether customers buy a Kindle Fire or use Amazon apps on an iPad. He compares Bezos favorably to Jobs, not for copying Apple, but for doggedly pursuing his own unique vision.
key_points: Amazon's event was specifically designed for tech press, exploiting their natural aversion to lock-in, high prices, and dominant market leaders to position the Kindle Fire against the iPad
Bezos's 'we want to make money when people use our devices, not when they buy our devices' framing simultaneously explains lower prices without seeming cheap and casts Apple's margins as greedy

The Kindle Fire is limited to countries where Amazon has content deals because without media to sell, Amazon can't recoup its razor-thin hardware margins — it's an accelerator for Amazon.com consumption, not a standalone product
Amazon has a hedge strategy: they win whether you buy a Kindle Fire or an iPad with Amazon apps, while Apple only wins if you buy the iPad
Bezos is Jobs-like not because he copied Jobs, but because he doggedly pursues a unique vision year after year in the name of customer satisfaction
money_quotes: Amazon is, to my eyes, the only company playing in the same league as Apple.
We want to make money when people use our devices, not when they buy our devices.
Tech writers have a natural aversion to lock-in, high prices, and dominant market leaders. As reflexes, these aversions are healthy. As dogma, they are not.
It's a heads we win, tails you lose strategy. That's the brilliance.
Amazon wins so long as you consume media content from Amazon, no matter if you play it on a Kindle Fire or an iPad. Apple only wins if you buy an iPad.
The Fire is more like an accelerator — it's a vehicle for turning active Amazon.com customers into even more active Amazon.com customers.
What he's done that is Jobs-like is doggedly pursue, year after year, iteration after iteration, a vision unlike that of any other company — all in the name of making customers happy.
key_insight: Amazon's Kindle Fire strategy is brilliantly asymmetric — they profit whether you buy their tablet or use Amazon apps on a competitor's, while Apple only wins if you buy the iPad.
